items.title: 3CXDesktopApp Intrusion Campaign Prevention
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The malicious activity involved trojanized signed installers that deployed a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. Analysis revealed the attack utilized
items.ai_summary: The 3CXDesktopApp intrusion campaign involves a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The attack utilizes trojanized signed installers that deploy a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. This campaign is attributed to a highly skilled threat actor, with TTPs detected including T1608 for stage capabilities and T1195 for supply chain compromise. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove any installed 3CXDesktopApp instances and replace them with a clean version, ensuring all dependencies like ffmpeg are updated to the latest versions. Additionally, network traffic should be monitored for suspicious HTTPS beacons to known command and control servers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1608| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An unknown actor distributes malicious VBS scripts via WhatsApp
items.description: An active malware campaign has been discovered distributing malicious VBScript files through WhatsApp direct messages since June 2026. The operation affects users across multiple countries, with Malaysia experiencing the highest concentration of victims. Attackers compromise WhatsApp accounts and send weaponized VBS files disguised as business and financial documents to contacts. The multi-stage i
items.ai_summary: The malicious VBS scripts are distributed through compromised WhatsApp accounts, with the attackers sending disguised business and financial documents to contacts. The scripts utilize Visual Basic (T1059.005) to execute malicious code, and the operation involves multi-stage channels (T1104) to evade detection. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, with users across multiple countries affected, particularly in Malaysia. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict file-type restrictions on WhatsApp and other messaging platforms, and ensure that all users are aware of the risks associated with opening suspicious documents from unknown sources. Additionally, defenders can monitor for unusual patterns of VBS file execution and implement controls to restrict the execution of VBS files from untrusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.005| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Detecting the Klue supply chain attack in Salesforce instances
items.description: On June 11, 2026, the Icarus threat group compromised Klue's backend systems, a market intelligence platform used by hundreds of enterprises to sync competitive battlecard data with CRM environments. The attackers exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to harvest OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong. Through automated API calls using Python scripts, the group exfilt
items.ai_summary: The Klue supply chain attack, attributed to the Icarus threat group, exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to harvest OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong. This attack, detected on June 11, 2026, affected hundreds of enterprises using Klue's market intelligence platform to sync competitive battlecard data with CRM environments. The attackers used automated API calls via Python scripts to exfiltrate data, highlighting the importance of securing abandoned integrations and monitoring for suspicious API activity. To remediate, defenders should immediately review and revoke any unused or dormant credentials, particularly those related to prototype integrations, and implement strict OAuth token management, such as short-lived tokens and regular token rotation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their Salesforce and Gong instances for unusual API call patterns and implement rate limiting to prevent automated exfiltration attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: PHISH ALERT: From a Simple Phishing Email to a Full Attack Arsenal: The Evolution of "ClickFix"
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign leverages evolved ClickFix techniques to bypass modern endpoint security through victim-assisted execution. Targets receive emails with urgent OneDrive document lures containing malicious ZIP attachments. The attack uses LNK shortcuts that redirect victims to landing pages, silently injecting PowerShell commands into their clipboard. Through social engineering, vi
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix phishing campaign utilizes sophisticated techniques to bypass endpoint security, leveraging victim-assisted execution to inject malicious PowerShell commands into the victim's clipboard. The attack begins with an urgent email containing a malicious ZIP attachment, which redirects the victim to a landing page via LNK shortcuts. If exploited, the victim's system may be compromised, allowing the threat actor to execute arbitrary commands, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should ensure their systems are configured to block LNK shortcuts from executing malicious code and implement clipboard content validation to prevent PowerShell command injection. Additionally, users should be educated on the risks of interacting with suspicious emails and attachments, and endpoint security solutions should be updated to detect and prevent ClickFix techniques.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1115| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Artifact scanner detects npm package 'node-fetch-utils' using external dependency resolution with remote tarball dependency from GitHub
items.description: A malicious npm package named 'node-fetch-utils' was discovered masquerading as a legitimate fetch helper utility. The package declares a remote tarball dependency from GitHub that executes upon installation. It runs an obfuscated postinstall script targeting Windows systems, which downloads a bundled Python runtime and drops it as Microsoft\EdgeBroker\pythonw.exe for persistence. The dropper then
items.ai_summary: The 'node-fetch-utils' npm package has been found to be malicious, utilizing external dependency resolution to download a remote tarball from GitHub, which executes upon installation. This package targets Windows systems, running an obfuscated postinstall script that downloads a bundled Python runtime, dropping it as MicrosoftEdgeBrokerpythonw.exe for persistence. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for unauthorized access and data breaches, affecting developers and organizations that have installed this package. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the 'node-fetch-utils' package and inspect their systems for any suspicious files or activity, particularly the MicrosoftEdgeBroker directory. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and avoid using packages with suspicious or obfuscated scripts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195.001| B[Compromise Software Dependencies]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Masquerading]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Unintended Proxy]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From PostCSS Masquerading to Windows RAT
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack leverages typosquatting of the legitimate postcss-selector-parser npm package, which receives over 150 million weekly downloads. Three malicious packages published by user 'abdrizak' masquerade as PostCSS utilities while delivering a multi-stage Windows RAT. The infection chain begins with encoded JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, which then download a b
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a sophisticated supply chain attack leveraging typosquatting of the legitimate postcss-selector-parser npm package, which has over 150 million weekly downloads. The malicious packages, published by user 'abdrizak', masquerade as PostCSS utilities while delivering a multi-stage Windows RAT. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to a full compromise of the affected system, allowing threat actors to gain remote access and execute arbitrary commands. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove any packages published by 'abdrizak' and update their npm package lists to prevent reinstallation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious PowerShell activity and encoded JavaScript files, and consider implementing a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar supply chain attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Customer CRM Data Accessed in Supply Chain Incident
items.description: LastPass experienced a security incident through Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with its Salesforce and Gong systems. On June 12, 2026, LastPass was notified that an unauthorized actor exploited stolen OAuth tokens held by Klue to access customer relationship management data within LastPass's Salesforce environment. The exposed information includes customer names, emai
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited stolen OAuth tokens held by Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with LastPass's Salesforce and Gong systems, to access customer relationship management data. The attack vector involved exploiting public-facing applications, specifically targeting the supply chain, as indicated by the detection of T1190 and T1195 tactics. The exposed information includes customer names and email addresses, highlighting the potential for phishing and social engineering attacks. To remediate, defenders should immediately review and revoke any compromised OAuth tokens, ensure that all integrations with third-party services are thoroughly vetted, and implement additional security controls such as multi-factor authentication for all users accessing sensitive data. Furthermore, defenders should monitor their systems for any suspicious activity related to T1555.005, indicating potential password manager exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.005| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The #APT36 cluster can't stop, won't stop
items.description: They just added #CVE-2026-21509 and #CVE-2026-21513 (borrowed from APT28) onto their delivery chain, pushing updated FIREPOWER via weaponized RTF and LNKs against 🇮🇳 targets. Separately, fresh SheetCreep + a shiny new CrystalShell-Slack variant co-dropped on a Kashmir target, because one implant is never enough. The vibeware factory is running three shifts: Crystal, .NET and PowerShell.
items.ai_summary: The APT36 cluster has been observed utilizing CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513 in their delivery chain, leveraging weaponized RTF and LNK files to target Indian entities. The attack vector involves the exploitation of these vulnerabilities to gain initial access, followed by the execution of PowerShell scripts, as indicated by the detection of T1059.001. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information, with targets in the Kashmir region being affected. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their systems are updated with the latest security patches, specifically focusing on the vulnerabilities mentioned, and implement robust email account security measures to prevent T1586.002 tactics. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious PowerShell activity and implementing a least-privilege approach can help mitigate the risk of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Multi-Stage Steganographic Loader Campaign Deploying Diverse Payloads Globally
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign was identified distributing multiple malware families through a multi-stage loader utilizing steganography and fileless techniques. The infection chain begins with archive attachments containing files disguised as financial documents, primarily targeting Indian organizations using names related to GST, NEFT, RTGS, and IMPS transactions. The loader employs in-memor
items.ai_summary: The threat campaign involves a multi-stage loader that utilizes steganography and fileless techniques to distribute diverse malware families globally, primarily targeting Indian organizations with archive attachments containing files disguised as financial documents. The infection chain begins with phishing emails using names related to GST, NEFT, RTGS, and IMPS transactions, exploiting the trust of recipients to gain initial access. If exploited, the malware can lead to data encryption and potential financial loss for affected organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block attachments with suspicious file types and names, and educate users to avoid opening emails with financial themes from untrusted sources. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for signs of obfuscated files or information and multi-stage channels, and consider implementing a security orchestration, automation, and response (SOAR) solution to streamline incident response.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Observed phishing URLs delivering RMM payload
items.description: ScreenConnect is used in phishing URLs to deliver RMM payload. DocuSign has been observed to be the common theme in these phishing emails.
items.ai_summary: The observed phishing campaign utilizes ScreenConnect to deliver a Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) payload, leveraging DocuSign as a common theme to trick victims into opening malicious emails. The attack vector involves phishing emails (T1566) that aim to gain initial access to the victim's system. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of the victim's system, potentially affecting individuals and organizations that use DocuSign services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about phishing attacks, and consider using anti-phishing tools that can detect and block suspicious emails. Additionally, ensuring that all systems and software are up-to-date with the latest security patches can help prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-12188: A vulnerability was detected in Grit42 Grit up to 0.11.0. Affected by this issue...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in Grit42 Grit up to 0.11.0. Affected by this issue is some unknown functionality of the file modules/core/backend/app/controllers/concerns/grit/core/grit_entity_controller.rb of the component GritEntityController. Performing a manipulation results in sql injection. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used. The vendor was contacte
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12188 is a SQL injection vulnerability in Grit42 Grit up to version 0.11.0, specifically affecting the GritEntityController component. The vulnerability can be exploited remotely, allowing attackers to manipulate the application's database. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized data access, modification, or deletion, potentially affecting users of the Grit42 Grit application. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Grit42 Grit version 0.11.1 or later, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, defenders should implement web application firewalls and intrusion detection systems to detect and prevent potential SQL injection attacks.
items.workflow_graph:

    B -->|T1055| C[Unmanaged API]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-12187: A security vulnerability has been detected in GL.iNet GL-MT3000 up to 4.4.5. Aff...
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in GL.iNet GL-MT3000 up to 4.4.5. Affected by this vulnerability is an unknown functionality of the file /usr/bin/one_click_upgrade of the component Online Firmware Upgrade Handler. Such manipulation leads to command injection. The attack can be launched remotely. The exploit has been disclosed publicly and may be used. Upgrading to version 4.7 addresses
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12187 is a command injection vulnerability in the GL.iNet GL-MT3000's Online Firmware Upgrade Handler, affecting versions up to 4.4.5. An attacker can remotely manipulate the /usr/bin/one_click_upgrade file, leading to arbitrary command execution. This vulnerability has been publicly disclosed, increasing the likelihood of exploitation. To remediate, upgrade to version 4.7 or later. Specifically, users should update to GL-MT3000 version 4.7 or later, which addresses this issue. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the Online Firmware Upgrade Handler is properly configured and monitored for suspicious activity.

items.title: CVE-2026-12186: A weakness has been identified in GL.iNet GL-MT3000 up to 4.4.5. Affected is the...
items.description: A weakness has been identified in GL.iNet GL-MT3000 up to 4.4.5. Affected is the function replace_country in the library /usr/lib/oui-httpd/rpc/tor of the component Tor Proxy Service Configuration Handler. This manipulation causes command injection. The attack can be initiated remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. Upgrading to version 4.7 is abl
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12186 is a command injection vulnerability in the GL.iNet GL-MT3000's Tor Proxy Service Configuration Handler, affecting versions up to 4.4.5. An attacker can exploit this weakness remotely, initiating a command injection attack. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands, potentially leading to a complete system compromise. To remediate this issue, users should upgrade to version 4.7 or later, ensuring they apply the latest security patches. Additionally, administrators should review and restrict access to the Tor Proxy Service Configuration Handler to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: CVE-2026-54413: driftregion iso14229 through 0.9.0 contains an integer underflow and downstream ...
items.description: driftregion iso14229 through 0.9.0 contains an integer underflow and downstream out-of-bounds read in the Handle_0x27_SecurityAccess() function in iso14229.c that allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to crash a UDS server and potentially read memory past the receive buffer by sending a single-byte 0x27 SecurityAccess request that follows any earlier well-formed 0x27 message. The handler reads
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54413 is a critical vulnerability in driftregion iso14229 versions 0.1.0 through 0.9.0, allowing a remote unauthenticated attacker to crash a UDS server and potentially read memory past the receive buffer by sending a specially crafted 0x27 SecurityAccess request. This is due to an integer underflow and downstream out-of-bounds read in the Handle_0x27_SecurityAccess() function in iso14229.c. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, or even ransomware attacks. To remediate, update to version 0.10.0 or later, and ensure that the iso14229.c file is properly patched. Additionally, consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) to block malicious traffic.

items.title: CVE-2026-54412: LiamBindle MQTT-C through version 1.1.6 contains a heap-based out-of-bounds read...
items.description: LiamBindle MQTT-C through version 1.1.6 contains a heap-based out-of-bounds read and integer underflow in the mqtt_unpack_publish_response() function in src/mqtt.c that allows a remote unauthenticated attacker controlling an MQTT broker - or able to inject MQTT traffic into an unencrypted session - to crash a subscribed MQTT-C client and potentially disclose adjacent heap memory by sending a singl
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54412 is a heap-based out-of-bounds read and integer underflow vulnerability in LiamBindle MQTT-C through version 1.1.6. A remote unauthenticated attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious MQTT packet to a subscribed MQTT-C client, potentially crashing the client and disclosing adjacent heap memory. This vulnerability is exploitable through an MQTT broker or by injecting MQTT traffic into an unencrypted session. To remediate this vulnerability, update to version 1.1.7 or later, and ensure that MQTT traffic is encrypted. Additionally, consider implementing rate limiting and access controls to prevent abuse.

The overflow corrupts the adjacent buffer-index field of the nanoMODBUS state stru

items.title: CVE-2026-11527: Config::IniFiles versions before 3.001000 for Perl allow OS command injection an...
items.description:
Config::IniFiles versions before 3.001000 for Perl allow OS command injection and file overwrite via a 2-arg open() of the -file argument in _make_filehandle.

Config::IniFiles::_make_filehandle opens a filename argument with Perl's 2-arg open(), so a filename that begins or ends with a pipe ("| cmd", "cmd |") or begins with a redirect ("> path", ">> path") is run as a command or redirect rather t

items.title: CVE-2026-11526: GD versions before 2.86 for Perl allow OS command injection and file overwrite v...
items.description:
GD versions before 2.86 for Perl allow OS command injection and file overwrite via a 2-arg open() of filename arguments in _make_filehandle.

GD::Image::_make_filehandle opens a filename argument with Perl's 2-arg open(), so a filename that begins or ends with a pipe ("| cmd", "cmd |") or begins with a redirect ("> path", ">> path") is run as a command or redirect rather than opened as a file. _ma

items.title: CVE-2025-15546: The Iptanus File Upload WordPress plugin before 5.1.7 does not implement proper ...
items.description: The Iptanus File Upload WordPress plugin before 5.1.7 does not implement proper file handling when the duplicatepolicy setting is configured to "maintain both." Due to a Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition between the file existence check and the actual file write operation, an authenticated attacker can overwrite files uploaded by other users.

items.description: Tata Electronics has confirmed in a statement to BleepingComputer that it was the target of a cyberattack that impacted parts of its IT infrastructure. [...]

items.title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
items.description: More victims have emerged after attackers breached application vendor Klue and used its OAuth tokens to steal customers' Salesforce data.

items.title: Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature
items.description: ​​Microsoft has released the KB5095093 preview cumulative update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, which fixes numerous bugs and begins rolling out new features, including the new Point-in-Time restore feature. [...]

items.title: Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people
items.description: Healthcare technology company Xsolis says that sensitive data belonging to nearly 1.4 million individuals was compromised in a phishing attack that gave attackers access to its network. [...]

items.title: 'Cordyceps': Mushrooming Malicious Pull Requests Threaten Developer Workflows
items.description: The CI/CD workflow weakness affects Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, Google's AI Agent Development Kit, Apache's Doris analytics database, Cloudflare's Workers SDK, and Python Software Foundation's Black.

items.title: Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity
items.description: "The timeline is not years, it is months,” the nations of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance said in a joint alert about the cybersecurity concerns of artificial intelligence.

items.title: New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer
items.description: A new macOS ClickFix campaign is using Terminal commands to silently download, mount, and launch info-stealing malware from malicious disk image (DMG) files. [...]

items.title: FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation
items.description: A Russian-speaking initial access broker (IAB) driven by financial gain is assessed to be behind a large-scale credential-harvesting operation known as FortiBleed that has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally. The campaign, active since February 2026, involves collecting credential lists, searching for exposed services, brute-forcing accessible systems, and deploying bespoke

items.title: Feds seize alleged cyber-scam infrastructure connected to Southeast Asian company
items.description: The Department of Justice announced the “seizure of a cloud computing account” used by subsidiaries of the Huione Group, a conglomerate severed from the U.S. financial system last year.

items.title: Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security
items.description: Named EmberAI, the new capability is built on Dragos’ massive operational technology cybersecurity dataset. The post Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trump directs federal agencies to protect US data from quantum threats
items.description: An executive order signed Monday aims to accelerate the government's transition to post-quantum cryptography (PQC), a new generation of encryption designed to protect data from the powerful quantum computers expected in the future.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
items.description: Attackers could abuse Dify's multi-tenant cloud service to read private chats, preview other tenants' documents, and reach internal APIs. The post Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London
items.description: Two members of the 'Scattered Spider' cybercrime group pleaded guilty to hacking the Transport for London (TfL) systems in 2024. [...]

items.title: Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission
items.description: The so-called duty of care provision that was excluded would have mandated that online platforms take reasonable measures to prevent specific harms such as suicidal ideation, eating disorders and cyberbullying by changing algorithm and design features.

items.title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents
items.description: Security firm AIR built a fake AI agent skill, pushed it through a popular skill marketplace and an Instagram ad, and says it reached roughly 26,000 agents, including some on corporate accounts. Every skill security scanner the firm tested it against marked it safe. The payload was harmless by design: it collected the user's email address and did nothing else. The point was to show

items.title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
items.description: President Trump signed an executive order on June 22 setting hard deadlines for federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography. Key establishment must move by December 31, 2030; digital signatures by December 31, 2031. EO 14409 leaves national security systems on a separate track. The deadlines matter because of a threat that does not

items.title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
items.description: GitHub is moving to strengthen software supply chain security by updating "actions/checkout" to block pwn request attacks that exploit the risky use of the "pull_request_target workflow" trigger to run malicious code with the workflow's full privileges. Effective June 18, 2026, the latest version of "actions/checkout," the official GitHub action for checking out a repository into the

items.title: The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You
items.description: Attackers can now weaponize newly disclosed vulnerabilities far faster than most organizations can patch them. Picus Security explains how security teams can validate exploitability before a public exploit even exists. [...]

items.title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
items.description: LastPass announced that hackers accessed customer data from its Salesforce environment after stealing the company's OAuth tokens in the Klue supply chain attack earlier this month. [...]

items.title: SocGholish Takedown Highlights Malicious TDS Threats
items.description: SocGholish uses traffic distribution systems (TDSs) to provide initial access into victims' networks for cybercrime groups such as the notorious Evil Corp.

items.title: Two Scattered Spider members plead guilty over cyberattack that crippled London transit
items.description: A 20-year-old and an 18-year-old admitted to infiltrating the network of Transport for London in 2024, disrupting public transportation services for months.

items.title: Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks
items.description: The high-severity use-after-free vulnerability in Samsung's KNOX security framework affected Android-powered Galaxy devices from the S9 through S25. The post Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISO Conversations: Carl Froggett – Combining CISO and CIO at Deep Instinct
items.description: Carl Froggett combines CISO and CIO. He currently occupies both positions at Deep Instinct. Before then, he was CISO at Citi for almost 17 years. The post CISO Conversations: Carl Froggett – Combining CISO and CIO at Deep Instinct appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
items.description: The threat actors engineered a Golang-based sniffer to target 430,000 FortiGate firewalls and identify 110 million credentials in the ongoing global campaign.

items.title: Webinar: Why email security teams are drowning in alerts
items.description: Phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks continue to overwhelm security teams with alerts and investigations. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help automate detection and response workflows, reducing alert fatigue and improving operational efficiency. [...]

items.title: Algerian Man Extradited to US for Running Cybercrime Marketplaces
items.description: 26-year-old Abdellah Belmili faces up to 30 years in prison for allegedly operating the marketplaces Market0Day and Spoxy. The post Algerian Man Extradited to US for Running Cybercrime Marketplaces appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances
items.description: Attackers can send crafted media files to execute code in any application that uses FFmpeg’s libavcodec library. The post FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior
items.description: Every weapon begins as an extension of the hand that holds it. The spear lengthened the reach of the arm. The bow sent the point flying without the throw. The rifle placed a man's death a quarter mile beyond his sight, and the aircraft carried that death across oceans. At each turn, the distance between the warrior and the wound grew wider, and yet one thing never moved: a human chose the target

items.title: OpenAI Refocuses Cybersecurity Efforts on Patching Over Discovery
items.description: OpenAI has expanded its Daybreak cybersecurity initiative with a new suite of tools and partnerships. The post OpenAI Refocuses Cybersecurity Efforts on Patching Over Discovery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign
items.description: Using a custom sniffer, the threat actor has captured over 110 million credentials since at least February 2026. The post Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a set of malicious npm packages that are designed to deliver a Windows-based remote access trojan (RAT). The list of identified packages, is below - aes-decode-runner-pro (145 downloads) postcss-minify-selector (256 downloads) postcss-minify-selector-parser (615 downloads) All the packages were published over the past month by an npm user named

items.title: WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool
items.description: Direct messages sent via WhatsApp are being used to distribute malicious Visual Basic Script (VBScript) files that lead to the installation of legitimate Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) software. Per findings from Kaspersky, the active campaign is targeting users of WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web across Malaysia, Brazil, India, Mexico, Singapore, the U.K., Spain, Taiwan, Australia,

items.title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
items.description: OpenAI on Monday said it's releasing an improved version of its GPT‑5.5‑Cyber model to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative the artificial intelligence (AI) company announced last month. Calling GPT‑5.5‑Cyber its "strongest model yet for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities," OpenAI said the model can "sustain deeper analysis across large codebases" to

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
items.description: An ongoing malware campaign is targeting WhatsApp users in multiple countries with deceptive messages that push VBScript files, leading to remote system access. [...]

items.title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
items.description: Four vulnerabilities allow attackers to exploit Dify, a platform for AI application building and management, to silently access and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Multiple WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack after unknown threat actors managed to tamper with the official release channels and push backdoor code. "Attackers compromised the vendor's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro plugin releases distributed through official licensed update channels," Wordfence said in an analysis

items.title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
items.description: A heap over-read in the Squid web proxy can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including any credentials or session tokens it carries, to anyone already allowed to send traffic through the same proxy. The bug traces to a 1997 FTP-parsing change and is still live in Squid's default configuration. Researchers at Calif.io disclosed it in June and named it Squidbleed (

items.title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
items.description: Attackers are using multiple online channels — including GitHub, YouTube, and VirusTotal — to build an illusion of trust to spread a cross-platform clipboard hijacker.

items.title: He Thought He Was Secure; His Phone Number Got Stolen Anyway
items.description: Threat actors can easily steal one-time passwords sent by text when they conduct a SIM swap attack. This can lead to account takeovers, so users must layer up their security measures.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 22nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9980, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Highlights Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
